title: Acer campestre subsp. marsicum
what_description: Acer campestre subsp. marsicum is a subspecies of the green field maple, belonging to the family Sapindaceae. It is characterized by its deciduous nature, with leaves that are 5-9 lobed, serrated, and typically 5-10 cm long, often having 7 leaflets. The leaves are dark green above and pale green below, with petioles 1-2 cm long. The flowers are greenish-yellow, in clusters of 2-5, and borne in spring, while the fruit is a samara, 2-3 cm long, with a wing that is 1-2 cm long. The tree grows up to 20 m tall, with a trunk diameter of up to 60 cm.
ailments_description: The most common ailments you can heal with Acer campestre subsp. marsicum are inflammation, which can be reduced by its anti-inflammatory properties, and diabetes, whose symptoms can be managed by the plant's antidiabetic activity. Additionally, this plant has been used to treat arthritis due to its analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties. Its antitumor activity also shows promise in the treatment of cancer. Acer campestre subsp. marsicum has anxiolytic properties, making it effective in reducing anxiety. Furthermore, it can be used to treat rheumatism, hypertension, digestive issues, gastroenteritis, diarrhea, fever, skin irritation, skin infections, gout, and wounds, while also alleviating stress, pain, cough, and respiratory problems, and aiding in wound healing.
properties_description: The main therapeutic properties of Acer campestre subsp. marsicum are multifaceted, including its antioxidant properties, which are attributed to the presence of flavonoids and phenolic acids that help mitigate oxidative stress and protect cells from damage. The plant's anti-inflammatory properties are also noteworthy, as they are mediated by saponins and terpenoids that inhibit pro-inflammatory enzymes and cytokines, thereby reducing inflammation and promoting tissue repair. Additionally, Acer campestre subsp. marsicum exhibits antimicrobial properties, which are primarily due to the presence of volatile oils and phenolic compounds that inhibit the growth of microorganisms and prevent infections. Furthermore, the cardioprotective properties of the plant are largely attributed to the flavonoids and saponins that help lower blood pressure, improve lipid profiles, and reduce the risk of cardiovascular disease. Its analgesic properties are also significant, as they are mediated by the presence of saponins and phenolic compounds that inhibit pain mediators and reduce pain perception, providing relief from various types of pain.
constituents_description: The most important healing constituents of Acer campestre subsp. marsicum are quercetin, a potent antioxidant flavonoid with anti-inflammatory properties, tannins, which exhibit antimicrobial and astringent effects, flavonoids, including kaempferol, a flavonoid with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities, and phenolic acids, such as gallic and ellagic acids, which have antimicrobial and antioxidant effects, contributing to the plant's potential therapeutic benefits in treating various health conditions, including cardiovascular and inflammatory diseases.
preparations_description: The main herbal preparations of Acer campestre subsp. marsicum are infusion, tea, tincture, salve, and decoction. Infusion and decoction are prepared by steeping the leaves and bark in hot water, the latter typically requiring longer steeping time. Tincture is a concentrated liquid extract obtained through the process of maceration in a solvent such as ethanol or glycerol. Salve, a topical preparation, is created by infusing the herbal material in a carrier oil, often combined with beeswax, to be used for skin issues like wounds or inflammation. These preparations are typically used to treat various health conditions, including digestive issues, fever, and inflammation, due to the presence of bioactive compounds like flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids in Acer campestre subsp. marsicum.
side_effects_description: The possible side effects of using Acer campestre subsp. marsicum improperly include inducing nausea and causing diarrhea, likely due to its toxicity and the presence of saponins and other bioactive compounds that can disrupt normal gastrointestinal function. Additionally, improper use of Acer campestre subsp. marsicum can exacerbate allergic reactions in individuals who are sensitive to its sap or other plant components, leading to an increased severity of symptoms such as hives, itching, and difficulty breathing. Furthermore, the sap of Acer campestre subsp. marsicum contains allergenic compounds that can develop skin irritation and trigger allergic reactions in susceptible individuals, characterized by redness, swelling, and blistering of the skin. This is because the sap contains a mixture of chemical compounds that can cause an immune response, leading to inflammation and irritation. The improper use of Acer campestre subsp. marsicum can also lead to severe and potentially life-threatening allergic reactions in rare cases, emphasizing the need for caution and proper handling.
